summary: French psychoanalyst who theorized the 'mirror stage' (infants recognizing their reflection as key to ego formation), divided reality into the Imaginary, Symbolic, and Real orders, called for a 'return to Freud,' and published his work in the Ecrits. Influential on film theory (Laura Mulvey's 'male gaze'), queer theory, and political philosophy (Slavoj Zizek).

works.name: The Mirror Stage and Ego Formation
works.description: Lacan's foundational concept: the 'mirror stage' occurs when infants (6-18 months) first recognize their reflection, developing apperception and forming the ego. The infant simultaneously recognizes the reflection as both itself and not-itself. The ego is a meconnaissance (misrecognition). This creates a pre-language concept of self and is key to entering the Imaginary order. The concept is presented in his Ecrits.

clues.clue: Mirror stage: infants recognize reflection, forming the ego
examples: theorized the 'mirror stage'
posited a time in which a child can picture themselves outside of themselves called 'the mirror stage'

clues.clue: Ego as meconnaissance (misrecognition); pre-language concept of self
examples: presents the ego as a meconnaisance in this stage
simultaneously recognizing a reflection as both itself and not-itself

clues.clue: Occurs at 6-18 months; develops apperception
examples: 6-to-18-month-olds could first recognize their reflection
babies perceive of themselves through an object that is able to induce apperception

works.name: The Three Orders: Imaginary, Symbolic, and Real
works.description: Lacan divided reality into three registers: the Imaginary (related to the mirror stage and ego), the Symbolic (language, law, the phallus as signifier), and the Real (that which resists symbolization). He portrayed these as a Borromean Knot. The Real roughly corresponds to Freud's id. Julia Kristeva's Powers of Horror argues that the 'abject' is experienced when the Real erupts into our lives. Zizek defines ideology in terms of Lacan's 'fantasies' explaining loss of the 'sublime object.'

clues.clue: Three orders/registers: Imaginary, Symbolic, Real
examples: divided reality into the Imaginary, Real, and Symbolic orders
his three registers as a Borromean Knot

clues.clue: Real roughly corresponds to Freud's id
examples: Lacan's Real order roughly corresponds to this concept from Freudian terminology

clues.clue: Powers of Horror: 'abject' erupts from Real
examples: the 'abject' is experienced when one of this thinker's concepts erupts into our lives

clues.clue: Phallus as signifier; sexuation upon entering symbolic order
examples: borrowed structuralist ideas to argue that the phallus is a signifier
criticized his account of sexuation upon entrance to the symbolic order

works.name: Objet Petit a, Jouissance, and Mathemes
works.description: The objet petit a is Lacan's term for the object-cause of desire, related to his concept of 'surplus jouissance' (paralleling Marx's surplus value). Jouissance describes the intense pleasure and pain felt as the Subject futilely tries to recapture the Object. His 'mathemes' are pseudo-mathematical symbols (e.g., $ <> a for 'fantasy') used to formalize psychoanalytic concepts. He identified four discourses: the master, the university, the analyst, and the hysteric. His seminars at Sainte-Anne Hospital ran from 1954-1976, edited by Jacques-Alain Miller.

clues.clue: Objet petit a: object-cause of desire; related to surplus jouissance
examples: formulated process... through the use of an objet petit a
paralleled Marx's concept of surplus value with his concept of 'surplus jouissance'

clues.clue: Jouissance: intense pleasure and pain in futile attempt to recapture Object
examples: this French term to describe the intense pleasure and pain felt by the Subject
concept of jouissance was embraced by feminists

clues.clue: Mathemes: pseudo-mathematical symbols ($ <> a = 'fantasy'); graph of desire
examples: used a dollar sign, a less than sign, a greater than sign, followed by the letter a, to create the 'matheme'
described his ideas in formulas known as 'mathemes'

clues.clue: Four discourses: master, university, analyst, hysteric
examples: argued that there are four basic discourses, called 'the master,' 'the university,' 'the analyst,' and 'the hysteric'

clues.clue: Seminars at Sainte-Anne Hospital; edited by Jacques-Alain Miller
examples: The Sainte-Anne Hospital hosted some of this thinker's yearly seminars
Jacques-Alain Miller edited these works

works.name: Seminar on Poe's 'The Purloined Letter'
works.description: Lacan's influential seminar analyzing Poe's 'The Purloined Letter,' arguing that the title document is a signifier of the unknown, and that it itself is a character whose presence-absence allows events to unfold.

clues.clue: Seminar on Poe's Purloined Letter: title document as signifier of the unknown
examples: argued that the title document is a signifier of the unknown in his seminar on Poe's The Purloined Letter
says the title object is the presence-absence that allows event to unfold

works.name: General / Biographical and Influence
works.description: French psychoanalyst who called for a 'return to Freud' and published work in his Ecrits. Pioneered variable-length sessions (IPA stripped him of recognition). Took his reading of Hegel from teacher Alexander Kojeve, calling Hegel 'the most sublime hysteric.' Influenced Laura Mulvey's concept of the 'male gaze' (from his concept of the 'gaze,' using Holbein's The Ambassadors). Slavoj Zizek frequently applies Lacan to political questions (The Pervert's Guide to Cinema). Elizabeth Grosz wrote a 'Feminist Introduction' to him. Feminists Irigaray and Kristeva embraced jouissance while critiquing sexuation. Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick suggested moving past Lacanian notions. Felix Guattari developed schizoanalysis to improve upon Lacan's work. His analysis of Las Meninas disagreed with Foucault's in The Order of Things. Lee Edelman coined 'sinthomosexual' referencing Lacan's concept of sinthome.

clues.clue: Ecrits is the main publication; published work collected there
examples: posited the 'mirror stage' in works like his Ecrits

examples: used the example of the anthropomorphic skull in Holbein's The Ambassadors

clues.clue: His analysis of Las Meninas disagreed with Foucault's
examples: Lacan's analysis of the painting Las Meninas disagreed with the analysis of Las Meninas presented at the opening of this Frenchman's The Order of Things

comprehensive_summary:
Jacques Lacan is a French psychoanalyst best known for theorizing the 'mirror stage,' dividing reality into the Imaginary, Symbolic, and Real orders, and calling for a 'return to Freud.' His work is published primarily in his Ecrits.

The mirror stage is Lacan's foundational concept: it occurs when infants (aged 6-18 months) first recognize their reflection, forming the ego through apperception. The infant simultaneously recognizes the reflection as itself and not-itself, making the ego a meconnaissance (misrecognition). This creates a pre-language concept of self and initiates the Imaginary order.

Lacan's three orders — Imaginary, Symbolic, and Real — form a Borromean Knot. The Real roughly corresponds to Freud's id and resists symbolization. Julia Kristeva's Powers of Horror argues the 'abject' erupts when the Real intrudes. The Symbolic involves language, law, and the phallus as a signifier. His concept of sexuation upon entering the symbolic order was critiqued by feminists like Irigaray and Kristeva.

The objet petit a is the object-cause of desire, connected to 'surplus jouissance' (paralleling Marx's surplus value). Jouissance describes intense pleasure and pain in the futile attempt to recapture the Object. Lacan formalized these concepts with 'mathemes' — pseudo-mathematical symbols. He identified four discourses (master, university, analyst, hysteric). His seminars at Sainte-Anne Hospital ran from 1954-1976 and were edited by Jacques-Alain Miller.

His seminar on Poe's 'The Purloined Letter' argues the title document is a signifier of the unknown whose presence-absence drives events. He influenced Laura Mulvey's concept of the 'male gaze' and used Holbein's The Ambassadors to analyze the 'gaze.' Slavoj Zizek frequently applies Lacan to political and cultural questions. He was stripped of IPA recognition for variable-length sessions. He took his Hegel reading from Kojeve and called Hegel 'the most sublime hysteric.' Felix Guattari developed schizoanalysis to improve on his methods. Lee Edelman coined 'sinthomosexual' from Lacan's sinthome concept.

cards.front: Concept: Lacan's three registers — the order of images and ego, the order of language and law, and that which resists symbolization — portrayed as a Borromean Knot

cards.front: Concept: the object-cause of desire, related to 'surplus jouissance' (paralleling Marx's surplus value)
cards.work: Objet Petit a, Jouissance, and Mathemes

cards.front: Concept: pseudo-mathematical symbols like $ <> a (for 'fantasy') used to formalize psychoanalytic concepts; this thinker also identified four discourses (master, university, analyst, hysteric)
cards.work: Objet Petit a, Jouissance, and Mathemes

cards.front: Work: a seminar arguing that the title document from a Poe story is a signifier of the unknown whose presence-absence drives the plot
Philosopher: Laura Mulvey developed 'the male gaze' from this thinker's concept of the 'gaze,' which this thinker illustrated using the anamorphic skull in Holbein's The Ambassadors
Philosopher: Slavoj Zizek frequently applies this thinker to political questions, including in The Pervert's Guide to Cinema and The Sublime Object of Ideology
Philosopher: was stripped of IPA recognition for variable-length therapy sessions; took his reading of Hegel from Alexander Kojeve
Philosopher: Felix Guattari developed schizoanalysis to improve on this thinker's psychoanalytic work
